relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Moses and Aaron speak liberation as festival-worship in the wilderness.
Pharaoh answers from ignorance and refusal: Who is YHWH?
The Divine of the Hebrews calls them toward wilderness sacrifice.
Pharaoh hears worship as loosening from labor.
Rest itself threatens the empire's burden-system.
That same day oppression is administratively increased.
Straw is withheld, but production remains demanded.
Pharaoh calls the cry for worship idleness.
Heavy service is used to drown out hope.
The command descends through taskmasters and foremen.
The people must search for straw without relief.
They scatter through Egypt for stubble.
The quota presses day by day.
Foremen are beaten for impossible demands.
They cry to Pharaoh against injustice.
The fault is named upward, not in the workers.
Pharaoh repeats the accusation of idleness.
No straw, same bricks: empire's cruelty made plain.
The foremen see themselves trapped in harm.
They meet Moses and Aaron outside Pharaoh's presence.
Their pain turns toward the messengers of liberation.
Moses returns to YHWH with honest anguish.
The first obedience has not yet brought rescue, only intensified harm.

contemplative_note: Exodus 5 is the backlash chapter: when liberation speaks, empire tightens labor. Moses' honest grief becomes part of the holy process.
